description: Aristotle's most famous philosophical disagreement: his systematic rejection of Plato's Theory of Forms. Aristotle's objection, known as the 'Third Man Argument', runs: if particulars are beautiful because they participate in the Form of Beauty, then the Form of Beauty and the particular beautiful things must share something in common — which would require a third entity to explain their commonality, and so on infinitely. More fundamentally, Aristotle argued that separating Forms from their instances explains nothing: how does a horse 'participate' in Horse-Form? Forms must be immanent in things, not transcendent above them. His own position — hylomorphism — holds that form and matter are inseparable, and that reality just is individual things with their actual constitutions and capacities.
